title: PIC — Cross-Domain Federation
description: A PIC transaction crosses an organizational trust boundary. Domain A (e.g., Partner) holds a PCA whose chain of trust ends at CAT_A's keys, which Domain B (e.g., Enterprise) does not natively trust. The Ingress Bridge at the boundary validates the incoming PCA against an inter-domain trust agreement, applies a domain-B policy that further restricts ops (a typical 'partners get read-only' rule), and re-anchors the transaction by minting PCA_int_0 against Domain B's CAT_B. Three invariants MUST hold: origin preservation (p_0 unchanged), monotonicity (ops_int ⊆ ops_external), and no expansion (the bridge cannot grant authority not justified by the incoming PCA). References pic-spec.md §6.5.2 (Federation Bridges).

steps.label: POST /v1/orders + PCA_A_n
steps.explanation: Domain A's egress executor E_A sends its request to Domain B's Ingress Bridge, attaching PCA_A_n. The PCA shows p_0=alice@partner-a.com, ops=(read:orders/*, write:orders/*), and signing CAT is CAT_A (kid=cat-a-kid-01). Domain B has no native trust relationship with CAT_A — the Ingress Bridge will validate and re-anchor before forwarding internally.
annotations: The Ingress Bridge is THE policy enforcement point for incoming partner traffic. Nothing passes into Domain B until re-anchored.
E_A also includes X-PIC-Request-Sig — the body signed by E_A's key. The bridge verifies this to confirm the sender matches PCA_A_n.executor.binding before doing any policy work.
If the Ingress Bridge is unavailable, the partner cannot enter Domain B at all. This is a deliberate choke point — the natural place to apply rate limiting, threat detection, and DLP.

steps.label: GET /jwks (resolve CAT_A pubkey)

artifacts.raw_input:
PCA_A_n.header.kid = cat-a-kid-01
Target JWKS URL from inter-domain trust agreement:
  https://keys.partner-a.com/jwks.json
artifacts.signing_key_hint: No signing — this is a public-key fetch, used to verify PCA_A_n's signature.
artifacts.final_output:
CAT_A public key (Ed25519):
  kid = cat-a-kid-01
  x   = CAT_A_PUBLIC_KEY_BASE64URL

Used in next step to verify: PCA_A_n.signature

steps.label: 1. Inter-domain trust agreement
steps.value:
Domain B knows Partner A's JWKS URL because of a
pre-established inter-domain trust agreement:
  {
    partner_id: "partner-a.com",
    jwks_uri: "https://keys.partner-a.com/jwks.json",
    allowed_algorithms: ["EdDSA"],
    allowed_p_0_pattern: "*@partner-a.com",
    policy_ops: ["read:orders/*"]
  }

This agreement is the cryptographic embodiment of
'we trust Domain A's CAT to sign authority claims
for Domain A principals.'

Trust-anchor publication can also use DID resolution,
SPIFFE federation bundle endpoints, or static config.
The protocol does not prescribe; only that the bridge
has a verifiable, out-of-band route to the source keys.

steps.label: 2. Verify PCA_A_n signature
steps.value:
verify_signature(
  PCA_A_n_payload || PCA_A_n_protected_header,
  PCA_A_n_signature,          ← PCA_A_CAT_A_SIG
  CAT_A_public_key            ← fetched above
) → MUST be valid

Also verify temporal constraints:
  PCA_A_n.constraints.temporal.exp > now? ✓

Also verify executor binding matches sender:
  PCA_A_n.executor.binding
    = spiffe://partner-a.com/svc/egress
  TLS peer SVID or request sig key
    = spiffe://partner-a.com/svc/egress ✓

If any check fails → reject. Partner cannot enter.

steps.label: 3. Apply domain-B policy (monotonic intersection)
steps.value:
Incoming ops: (read:orders/*, write:orders/*)
Domain B policy for partner-a: (read:orders/*)

result = ops_in ∩ policy_allowed
       = (read:orders/*)

INVARIANT 1 (origin preservation):
  p_0 = alice@partner-a.com → PRESERVED verbatim.
  Domain B does NOT alias to a local user.

INVARIANT 2 (monotonicity):
  result ⊆ incoming ops ✓
  result ⊆ domain policy ✓

INVARIANT 3 (no expansion):
  Intersection semantics — even if domain policy
  nominally allowed admin:*, intersection with incoming
  caps it. Bridge CANNOT manufacture new authority.

Compose PCA_int_0 proposal:
  p_0 = alice@partner-a.com  (preserved)
  ops = (read:orders/*)      (intersected)
  exp = min(incoming.exp, now + 1800s)   (capped)

steps.explanation: The Ingress Bridge fetches CAT_A's JWKS to verify PCA_A_n's signature, then runs the three-invariant validation pipeline: verify signature → preserve p_0 → intersect ops with domain-B policy. The result is a proposal for PCA_int_0 that carries Alice's identity from Domain A, narrowed to what Domain B allows partners to do.
annotations: JWKS is aggressively cached (Cache-Control: 3600s here). Key rotation requires overlapping kids in the JWKS during cutover.
If the JWKS endpoint is unreachable, the bridge MUST NOT fail open — it MUST reject the request. Partners cannot enter without signature verification.
Governance integration is natural here: a PDP can be consulted at this exact step to apply per-tenant or per-purpose constraints atop the static inter-domain policy.

steps.label: POST /poc (re-anchor to CAT_B)
steps.explanation: The Ingress Bridge submits a federation-entry PoC to CAT_B. This is structurally a normal PoC — predecessor (the validated PCA_A_n), successor proposal, and the bridge's own attestation — but mode=federation-entry signals re-anchoring across a boundary rather than an internal hop. hop=0 in the successor is the cue. The bridge's SPIFFE SVID attests it is the authorized cross-domain entry point.
annotations: Only bridges whose attestation matches the CAT_B-registered 'authorized federation bridges' list can submit federation-entry PoCs. Arbitrary executors cannot re-anchor.
The predecessor PCA_A_n is included in full so CAT_B can see the complete cross-domain provenance for audit. CAT_B does not need to re-verify the Domain A signature — the bridge already did.
successor.provenance.cat.kid=cat-a-kid-01 is the audit back-reference — months later, Domain B can trace this internal authority back to a specific Domain A CAT signature.

steps.label: PCA_int_0 (signed by CAT_B)
steps.explanation: CAT_B mints PCA_int_0 — the root of the internal Domain B chain for this transaction. p_0 is preserved as alice@partner-a.com, ops is the intersected (read:orders/*), and provenance.cat back-references CAT_A's key so audit can reconstruct the full cross-domain chain. From this point, internal hops use the standard Causal Authority Transition flow against CAT_B.
annotations: hop=0 from CAT_B's perspective even though the overall transaction is multi-hop. The cross-domain link lives in provenance.cat, not the hop number.
p_0=alice@partner-a.com inside Domain B is intentional — audit, RBAC, and OPA policies can match partner principals directly without a synthetic local user mapping.
PCA_int_0.exp (30 min from now) is well inside PCA_A_n.exp (1 hour remaining). Internal authority cannot outlive the partner authority that birthed it.
provenance.executor.sig=INGRESS_POC_SIG captures WHICH bridge admitted this transaction — critical for incident response.

steps.label: Forward + PCA_int_0
steps.explanation: The Ingress Bridge forwards the request to E_B (the first internal Domain B executor) with PCA_int_0. From E_B's perspective this is a plain PCA from CAT_B — no cross-domain knowledge required at this step. E_B verifies PCA_int_0 against CAT_B, confirms ops permits the read, executes. The audit envelope shows full provenance: alice@partner-a.com → CAT_A → Ingress Bridge → CAT_B → order-api.
annotations: All cross-domain complexity ended at the bridge. E_B only sees a Domain B PCA — same logic it uses for any internal-origin request.
Subsequent internal hops (E_B calling another service) use plain Causal Authority Transition against CAT_B, monotonically restricting ops further.
On egress (response leaving Domain B back to Partner A), a symmetric Egress Bridge re-anchors Domain B PCAs into a Partner-A-trusted format. That mirror follows the same three invariants.
The cross_domain_origin audit block enables incident reconstruction months later: every read inside Domain B attributable to alice@partner-a.com is traceable to a specific signed PCA from CAT_A.
